one_line_takeaway: A small-scale, honestly-caveated comparison showing that QLoRA-fine-tuned 7B open LLMs (Llama/CodeLlama/Gemma/CodeGemma) are weak and unreliable C/C++ vulnerability detectors — CodeGemma's headline 87% recall / 58% F1 masks a below-chance 44% accuracy and the study's key baseline comparison is confounded by a dataset mismatch.
key_finding: Among the four fine-tuned emerging LLMs, CodeGemma attains the best Recall (87%) and F1 (58%) but the WORST accuracy (44%) — below chance on a balanced test set — indicating it over-predicts 'vulnerable' (precision only 43%). Llama 2 has the best accuracy among the four (65%); Gemma has the best precision (65%). The older 'state-of-the-art' models (GPT-2 Base, CodeGPT at 91% accuracy) dominate on accuracy but were, by the authors' own admission, evaluated on a DIFFERENT merged dataset (DiverseVul + two others) rather than the DiverseVul-only subset used for the new models, so the comparison is not apples-to-apples. The authors' honest headline conclusion is that strong general LLM performance does not transfer to reliable vulnerability detection, and that the models are unpredictable in output formatting.
core_contribution: An empirical comparative study of four 'emerging' open LLMs — Llama 2, CodeLlama, Gemma, and CodeGemma (all 7B) — fine-tuned via QLoRA for binary C/C++ source-code vulnerability detection on the DiverseVul dataset. The paper's own contribution is the head-to-head measurement of these newer models against each other (natural-language vs. code-specialized variants) and a comparison of those numbers against published results for older state-of-the-art models (GPT-2 Base, CodeBERT, CodeGPT). It does NOT propose a new model, architecture, dataset, or detection technique; the DiverseVul dataset and the BERT/GPT/RoBERTa/GPT-2 baselines all come from cited prior work, not from this paper.
threat_model: Defensive security tooling: automatically flagging whether a given C/C++ function contains a software security vulnerability (CWE-labeled), to help developers audit open-source code and inherited library dependencies. There is no adversarial/attacker threat model — the paper treats vulnerability detection as a supervised binary classification task, not as defense against an adversary manipulating the model.
limitations: 1) Baseline confound explicitly acknowledged by the authors: GPT-2 Base / CodeBERT / CodeGPT numbers are lifted from Chen et al. and were measured on a merged dataset, not the DiverseVul-only subset the new LLMs were tested on, undermining the central comparison. 2) Very small scale: fine-tuning on ~700–2000 examples and a test set of only 200 samples make all reported percentages statistically fragile. 3) Internal inconsistency in reported sample counts (1000/3000 vs. 700/2000). 4) CodeGemma's 44% accuracy vs. 87% recall reveals a degenerate near-'always-vulnerable' classifier, yet it is highlighted as the winner via F1/recall. 5) The 'test on unseen project' Table III is IDENTICAL to the same-dataset Table II for all four new LLM rows (only the borrowed baseline numbers change), so the paper does not actually demonstrate cross-project generalization of the fine-tuned LLMs. 6) Treating unparseable outputs as '0' biases toward the majority class and conflates model refusal with a prediction. 7) No statistical significance testing, no confidence intervals, single train/test split.

datasets_used: DiverseVul (Chen et al. 2023) — 349,437 C/C++ functions, 150 CWEs; reported imbalance 18,945 vulnerable vs. 311,547 non-vulnerable
Balanced subset of ~37k samples created via RandomUnderSampler (sampling_strategy=1)
Fine-tuning subsets: ~1000 (Llama) / ~3000 (Gemma) per Section III; ~700 / ~2000 per Challenges section, 80/20 split
Baseline numbers quoted from a 'Merged Diversvul' dataset (DiverseVul + two others) used in Chen et al. [1]
quantitative_results: CodeGemma (DiverseVul): Accuracy 44%, Precision 43%, Recall 87%, F1 58% — highest recall and F1 among the four new LLMs
Llama 2 (DiverseVul): Accuracy 65%, Precision 36%, Recall 17%, F1 25% — highest accuracy among the four new LLMs
Gemma (DiverseVul): Accuracy 63%, Precision 65%, Recall 23%, F1 33% — highest precision among the four new LLMs
GPT-2 Base (Merged DiverseVul, from [1]): Accuracy 91%, Precision 46%, Recall 25%, F1 33% (same-dataset test); Accuracy 95%, Precision 10%, Recall 5%, F1 6% (unseen-project test)
CodeBERT (Merged DiverseVul, from [1]): Accuracy 90%, Precision 39%, Recall 36%, F1 37% (same-dataset); Accuracy 94%, Precision 13%, Recall 10%, F1 11% (unseen-project)
CodeGPT (Merged DiverseVul, from [1]): Accuracy 91%, Precision 43%, Recall 29%, F1 35% (same-dataset); Accuracy 94%, Precision 10%, Recall 7%, F1 8% (unseen-project)
Fine-tuning ~15 min/model; testing ~1.5 hr/model; ~10 trials on average to tune parameters (NVIDIA A100, 80GB RAM / 40GB VRAM, Colab Pro)
baselines_compared: GPT-2 Base (results borrowed from Chen et al. [1], merged dataset)
Also cited as reference points but not tabulated with this paper's own runs: BERT, RoBERTa, GPT-3/GPT-4, PaLM, CodeGPT, GPT-2 base
